title: Towards Corpus-Grounded Agentic LLMs for Multilingual Grammatical Analysis

en.general:
This paper explores how advanced AI systems can help analyze grammar in languages around the world. Traditionally, studying grammar patterns in large collections of text (corpora) requires a lot of manual work and technical skills. The researchers propose using "agentic" large language models (LLMs) – AI systems that can reason, plan, and take action – to automate parts of this process.

The team built a framework where the AI interprets linguistic questions, writes code to analyze text data, and provides clear answers backed by evidence from the data. They tested this system on a well-known collection of annotated language data called Universal Dependencies (UD), which includes texts from over 170 languages. Their focus was on word-order patterns, such as whether a language typically places the subject before the verb or the object after the verb.

The evaluation looked at three key aspects: whether the AI correctly identified the dominant word order in a language, how thoroughly it covered different word-order variations, and how accurately it reflected the actual distribution of these patterns in real language data. The results show that combining AI reasoning with structured linguistic data is not only possible but also effective, opening the door to faster and more scalable grammatical research.

This work is a first step toward making complex grammatical analysis more accessible and automated, helping linguists and researchers study language patterns across many languages with less manual effort.
en.methodology:
The research design adopts an agentic LLM framework to automate multilingual grammatical analysis by reasoning over annotated corpora. The system integrates natural-language task interpretation, code generation, and data-driven reasoning to produce interpretable answers grounded in linguistic data. The framework is tested on Universal Dependencies (UD) corpora, focusing on word-order features inspired by the World Atlas of Language Structures (WALS). The evaluation covers 13 word-order features across over 170 languages, assessing performance through three dimensions: dominant-order accuracy, order-coverage completeness, and distributional fidelity.

The model/algorithm choices center on an agentic LLM architecture that decomposes tasks into subproblems, leveraging code generation for precise data extraction and analysis. The system interprets user queries in natural language, translates them into executable code (e.g., Python scripts) to query the UD corpora, and synthesizes results into interpretable reports. This approach balances flexibility in handling diverse linguistic queries with the rigor of structured data analysis.

Baselines for comparison include traditional rule-based methods and static LLM prompting without agentic capabilities. Rule-based systems lack adaptability to nuanced linguistic variations, while static LLMs may struggle with complex, multi-step reasoning tasks. The agentic framework is hypothesized to outperform these baselines by dynamically adapting to corpus-specific structures and providing transparent, data-grounded outputs.

The evaluation strategy employs three complementary metrics to assess system performance. Dominant-order accuracy measures the correctness of identified primary word-order patterns. Order-coverage completeness evaluates the system’s ability to detect all relevant word-order variations within a language. Distributional fidelity quantifies how accurately the system captures the frequency and distribution of these patterns. These dimensions collectively ensure a holistic assessment of the system’s generalization, comprehensiveness, and precision in grammatical analysis.

Assumptions include the availability of high-quality UD corpora with consistent annotations and the LLM’s ability to accurately interpret linguistic terminology. The framework assumes that word-order features can be reliably extracted from UD trees and that the agentic LLM can effectively bridge the gap between natural-language queries and structured data analysis. Limitations may arise from noise in corpus annotations or the LLM’s occasional misinterpretation of complex linguistic constructs.
en.dataset:
The dataset used in this study is derived from the Universal Dependencies (UD) corpora.

Language(s): The evaluation spans over 170 languages, covering multilingual grammatical tasks.

Source: The dataset is sourced from the Universal Dependencies (UD) project, a widely used framework for syntactic annotation of natural language corpora.

Size: The exact size of the dataset is not specified in the abstract, but it includes multiple annotated corpora across 170 languages, likely varying in size per language.

Modality: The primary modality is text, as the UD corpora consist of annotated linguistic data in the form of treebanks.

Labels: The dataset includes syntactic annotations such as part-of-speech tags, dependency relations, and morphological features, which are standard in UD treebanks.

Metadata: The metadata likely includes language identifiers, treebank names, and annotation guidelines, though specific details are not provided in the abstract.

Splits: The abstract does not explicitly mention dataset splits, but the evaluation involves testing on 13 word-order features across the languages, suggesting a task-specific partitioning of the data.
